questions.text: I trust my own judgment more than the group's consensus.
I resist starting new projects until current ones are at a natural stopping point.
I believe commitments should be flexible enough to accommodate new information.
I find that the felt weight of suffering is the experience that most reliably moves me from contemplation to engagement.
I find that my emotional system has no standby mode — it's either off or fully engaged.
I feel that personal recognition for my work is important to me.
I document lessons learned from past experiences to avoid repeating mistakes.
I believe my emotional steadiness is visible to others and often commented on.
I feel that the moral force of suffering is what keeps my ethical commitments grounded in reality rather than in abstraction.
I feel that the pull to respond to others' suffering is one of the few moral intuitions I trust absolutely.
I find that sharing credit publicly while absorbing blame privately is my natural style.
I tend to miss the social significance of things like seating arrangements or eye contact patterns.
I find it energizing to be part of a team that navigates challenges together.
I have consciously practiced maintaining emotional distance in situations where most people would be drawn in.
I believe my nervous system has a naturally high threshold for activation.
I find that I do not instinctively sense when someone feels overlooked in a group.
I find that my ability to remain analytically focused when confronted with pain has been a defining strength in my professional life.
I take pride in having built teams from scratch.
I tend to finish one task completely before starting another.
I notice when someone glances at the clock or door during a conversation, signaling they have mentally left.
I trust that things will come back to me without chasing them down.
I believe that the time I spend alone working on my goals is among my most valuable hours.
I think that the ability to step outside the emotional atmosphere of a situation is what makes it possible to see the most effective path forward.
I have a bias toward deliberation over premature action.
I hold the view that the surplus in my life exists so that I can fill the deficit in someone else's.
I do not usually notice when someone is nervous during a conversation unless they say so.
After a stressful event, it takes me a long time to return to my emotional baseline.
I notice when the physical distance between two people in a conversation is slightly greater than normal and read the reason for it.
I notice when someone's reply to a group message is really only intended for one person in the thread.
When I encounter injustice, my entire body lights up with agitation.
I am comfortable making commitments on behalf of my team without consulting them first.
I find that celebrations and exciting events feel pleasant without tipping into overwhelm.
I tend to measure my worth by how much I've contributed to others, not by what I've accumulated.
I take pride in the behind-the-scenes work that makes a project succeed.
I feel that acquiring resources for my own future is prudent and right.
I let the day's most important task reveal itself through my engagement rather than deciding it in advance.
